A1.label: Verified source count meeting disproof threshold

claim_formal.subject: quantum entanglement with pre-agreed measurement basis
claim_formal.property: number of independent authoritative sources confirming this is impossible
claim_formal.operator_note: This is a DISPROOF. The claim asserts entanglement enables FTL information transfer with a pre-agreed measurement basis. The no-communication theorem in quantum mechanics proves this is impossible: local measurement statistics are independent of the distant party's measurement choice, so no information can be encoded or decoded superluminally regardless of any pre-agreed protocol. We disprove the claim by finding >= 3 independent authoritative sources that confirm entanglement cannot transmit usable information FTL. The threshold of 3 reflects broad scientific consensus from independent institutions.

claim_natural: Quantum entanglement enables the transmission of usable information faster than the speed of light when the distant parties pre-agree on a measurement basis.

B1.quote: It asserts that during the measurement of an entangled quantum state, it is impossible for one observer to transmit information to another observer, regardless of their spatial separation.

B2.quote: Experiments have shown that this is not true, nor can quantum physics be used to send faster-than-light communications.

B3.quote: The current scientific consensus is that faster-than-light communication is not possible, and to date it has not been achieved in any experiment.

B4.source_name: QSNP (EU Quantum Flagship) — Debunking quantum myths
B4.quote: The catch is that we are not actually sending any information.

credibility.note: Unclassified domain — verify source authority manually

cross_checks.independence_note: Sources are from 4 different institutions/publications: Wikipedia (community encyclopedia citing physics literature), Caltech (university research institution), Wikipedia FTL article (separate article with distinct references), QSNP/EU Quantum Flagship (European research consortium). All trace to independent primary physics research and the mathematically proven no-communication theorem.

adversarial_checks.question: Is there any credible peer-reviewed physics paper demonstrating FTL information transfer via entanglement with pre-agreed measurement bases?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Searched for: 'pre-agreed measurement basis entanglement superluminal signaling loophole scheme'. Reviewed results from arXiv, Physics Forums, Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, and Wikipedia.
adversarial_checks.finding: No credible peer-reviewed source demonstrates FTL information transfer via entanglement. Proposed schemes (e.g., Gao Shan 2003) have been refuted. The no-communication theorem is mathematically proven under standard quantum mechanics. All search results confirm the impossibility.

adversarial_checks.question: Does pre-agreeing on a measurement basis create any loophole in the no-communication theorem?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Searched for: 'quantum entanglement FTL information transfer pre-agreed measurement basis impossibility'. Reviewed Physics Forums discussions and the arXiv paper 2001.08867.
adversarial_checks.finding: Pre-agreeing on a measurement basis does not create a loophole. The no-communication theorem proves that Bob's local measurement statistics are completely independent of Alice's measurement choice, regardless of any prior agreement. Each party sees random 50/50 outcomes locally; correlations only emerge when results are compared via a classical (light-speed-limited) channel.

adversarial_checks.question: Has any experiment ever demonstrated superluminal information transfer using quantum entanglement?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Searched for: 'quantum entanglement enables faster than light communication claim debunked physics'. Reviewed phys.org, Caltech, and QSNP articles.
adversarial_checks.finding: No experiment has ever demonstrated FTL information transfer. Wikipedia's Faster-than-light communication article states: 'The current scientific consensus is that faster-than-light communication is not possible, and to date it has not been achieved in any experiment.'
